title: Experimental demonstration of a coherent detector blinding attack on a real CV-QKD system
authors: Daniel Pereira, Vana Pezelj, Florian Prawits, Hannes Hübbel
abstract: Continuous-variable quantum key distribution provides a theoretical unconditionally secure solution to distribute symmetric keys among users in a communication network. However, the practical devices used to implement these systems are intrinsically imperfect, and, as a result, open the door to eavesdropper attacks. In this work, we present a novel implementation of a coherent detector blinding attack, in which the eavesdropper hinders the capability of the receiver to properly estimate the channel parameters, hiding the impact of their collective attack. Our results show that excess noise in excess of 2.5 SNU can be reliably hidden by the eavesdropper, thus demonstrating the feasibility of the attack. We also discuss how our attack strategy can be further improved to allow for even stronger attacks (by using more advanced modulation formats), and propose some countermeasures to prevent it.
summary: This paper demonstrates an experimental attack on a real CV-QKD system where an eavesdropper uses a blinding signal to saturate the receiver's detectors. By doing so, the attacker can hide significant amounts of added noise, making a large-scale interception appear as a secure channel. The study also identifies potential monitoring-based countermeasures to detect such attacks.
main_problem: The vulnerability of practical Continuous-Variable Quantum Key Distribution (CV-QKD) systems to side-channel attacks, specifically the ability of an eavesdropper to hide a collective attack by blinding the receiver's detector.
main_result: The authors experimentally demonstrated a coherent detector blinding attack that can reliably hide excess noise exceeding 2.5 Shot Noise Units (SNU) by forcing the receiver into a non-linear/saturated regime.
method: A two-stage attack strategy was implemented using a strong, detuned optical signal to induce detector saturation (enabling stage) and an EDFA-based noise source to simulate a collective attack (exploitation stage).
model_or_system: A real-world CV-QKD receiver architecture using a polarization-diverse assembly, balanced coherent detectors, and FPGA-based digital signal processing, operating over 15 km of standard single-mode fiber.
key_observables: Transmission (T), excess noise (epsilon), shot noise units (SNU), thermal noise, and detector output voltage saturation.
important_parameters: Blinding power levels (e.g., -14 dBm threshold), excess noise levels (up to 2.5 SNU), and the security threshold (0.126 SNU).
main_assumptions: The attack exploits the deviation of practical hardware from the ideal linear response assumed in standard security proofs; finite size effects were not considered.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the two-stage attack strategy; Figure 2 and 3 detail the blinding path and receiver architecture; Figure 4 shows the DSP stages; Figure 5 and 6 demonstrate the noise source and its reproducibility; Figure 7 shows the blinding source setup; Figure 12 illustrates the reduction of estimated excess noise as blinding power increases.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the security problem of CV-QKD, details the experimental setup for both the attack and the target receiver, describes the mathematical model of the blinding mechanism, presents experimental results showing noise hiding and detector saturation, and concludes with proposed countermeasures.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum communications as it explores the physical limits of security in practical photonic implementations and the impact of non-linear detector dynamics on quantum protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Diagram of the general attack strategy being considered in this paper.
Figure 2: Schematic diagram of the required path for the blinding light.

Figure 3: Diagram of the CV-QKD receiver being used as a target for the attack. ADC: Analog to Digital Converter; BS: 50/50 Beam Splitter; FPGA: Field Programmable Gate Array; OS: Optical Switch; PBS: Polarizing Beam Splitter; TEC: TEmperature Controller; TIA: Trans-Impedance Amplifier.
Figure 4: Diagram of the DSP stage of the CV-QKD receiver.

Figure 5: Diagram of the noise source used to simulate the impact of the collective attack. BS: 90/10 Beam Splitter; EDFA: Erbium-Doped Fibre Amplifier; OPM: Optical Power Meter; PSU: Power Supply Unit; VOA: Variable Optical Attenuator.
